teamObjective.explanation: Partner (player 2) has only 2 cards and is tagged near_finish; the best team goal is to lead a low single that partner can beat or that lets partner control the trick and finish their hand.

partnerBelief.summary: Partner likely holds one or two relatively strong cards intended to win a trick and go out, given their small hand count and the partner_near_finish tag; no public card play has occurred, so this remains probabilistic.

opponentBelief.summary: Opponents (players 1 and 3, with 3 cards each) likely hold enough variety that a very low lead may be beaten, but they have no public information about my hand and cannot know I am setting up partner.

actionRationale.primaryReason: Leading the lowest single (diamond 3) maximizes the chance that the partner can win the trick with a modest high card while preserving my slightly higher 4-6 diamonds for future turns if needed.

whyNotAlternatives.reason: Leading the highest of my four low cards reduces the gap opponents must cover to beat it and does not help partner more than the 3, while leaving the weaker 3-5 for later.
A 5 is still low but intermediate; it is more likely to be beaten by an opponent holding a 6 or higher than the 3, so it is a weaker setup card.
Similar to the 5, the 4 is slightly easier for opponents to overtake than the 3 and offers no compensating advantage since no straight flush or sequence is available.

risks: An opponent may play a higher single and take the trick, preventing partner from finishing.
If partner cannot beat even a 3, the lead may be wasted.
riskAssessment.mitigation: Leading the 3 gives the lowest possible bar for partner to win and conserves my remaining cards for a follow-up attempt if the trick is lost.

notes: All legal actions are low non-trump singles; no bomb or wildcard is available, so the decision is purely about which rank best supports the near-finish partner.
